question: What are the two basic coverage criteria for home blood glucose monitors?
expected_answer: 1) The beneficiary has diabetes (refer to ICD-10 code list in Policy Article), and 2) the treating practitioner has concluded the beneficiary has sufficient training using the device, evidenced by a prescription for supplies and frequency of testing.

question: What happens if basic coverage criteria (1)-(2) are not met for glucose monitors?
expected_answer: The item(s) will be denied as not reasonable and necessary.

question: What HCPCS codes are used for home blood glucose monitors with special features?
expected_answer: E2100 (integrated voice synthesizer) and E2101 (integrated lancing/blood sample). They are covered when basic criteria (1)-(2) are met and the treating practitioner certifies a severe visual impairment (20/200 or worse in both eyes).

question: Can code E2101 be covered for manual dexterity impairments?
expected_answer: Yes, E2101 is also covered for impairment of manual dexterity when basic criteria (1)-(2) are met and the treating practitioner certifies the impairment is severe enough to require use of this special monitoring system. Coverage for manual dexterity is not dependent upon visual impairment.

question: How many test strips and lancets are covered for a non-insulin-treated beneficiary under usual utilization?
expected_answer: Up to 100 test strips and up to 100 lancets every 3 months, if basic coverage criteria (1)-(2) are met.

question: How many test strips and lancets are covered for an insulin-treated beneficiary under usual utilization?
expected_answer: Up to 300 test strips and up to 300 lancets every 3 months, if basic coverage criteria (1)-(2) are met.

question: What are the criteria for high utilization of BGM testing supplies for non-insulin-treated beneficiaries?
expected_answer: More than 100 test strips and more than 100 lancets every 3 months are covered if: (a) basic criteria (1)-(2) are met, (b) within 6 months prior, the treating practitioner had an in-person or Medicare-approved telehealth visit to evaluate diabetes control and need for specific quantity, and (c) every 6 months the practitioner verifies adherence to the high utilization testing regimen.

question: What are the criteria for high utilization of testing supplies for insulin-treated beneficiaries?
expected_answer: More than 300 test strips and more than 300 lancets every 3 months are covered if criteria (a)-(c) are met: basic criteria, in-person/telehealth visit within 6 months, and every 6 months verification of adherence to high utilization regimen.

question: What are the 5 initial coverage criteria for a continuous glucose monitor (CGM)?
expected_answer: 1) Beneficiary has diabetes mellitus, 2) treating practitioner concluded sufficient training using the CGM as evidenced by prescription, 3) CGM prescribed in accordance with FDA indications, 4) CGM is being prescribed to improve glycemic control and beneficiary meets at least one of: (A) insulin-treated, or (B) history of problematic hypoglycemia, and 5) within 6 months prior to ordering, practitioner had an in-person or telehealth visit to evaluate diabetes control.

question: What qualifies as problematic hypoglycemia for CGM coverage criterion 4B?
expected_answer: Either: recurrent (more than one) level 2 hypoglycemic events (glucose <54mg/dL or 3.0mmol/L) that persist despite multiple attempts to adjust medications and/or modify the treatment plan; OR a history of one level 3 hypoglycemic event (glucose <54mg/dL) characterized by altered mental and/or physical state requiring third-party assistance for treatment.

question: What is the continued coverage requirement for CGM after initial prescription?
expected_answer: Every 6 months following the initial prescription, the treating practitioner must conduct an in-person or Medicare-approved telehealth visit with the beneficiary to document adherence to their CGM regimen and diabetes treatment plan.

question: What is the difference between adjunctive and non-adjunctive CGMs?
expected_answer: A non-adjunctive CGM can be used to make treatment decisions without the need for a stand-alone BGM to confirm testing results. An adjunctive CGM requires the user to verify glucose levels or trends displayed on the CGM with a BGM prior to making treatment decisions. As of February 28, 2022, CMS determined both types may be classified as DME.

question: What HCPCS codes are used for CGM devices?
expected_answer: E2102 for adjunctive, non-implanted CGM monitor or receiver; E2103 for non-adjunctive, non-implanted CGM monitor or receiver.

question: What are the supply allowance codes for CGMs and how are they billed?
expected_answer: A4238 for adjunctive CGM supply allowance, A4239 for non-adjunctive CGM supply allowance. Each includes all supplies and accessories, 1 month supply = 1 unit of service. Up to 3 UOS per 90 days may be billed. Billing more than 3 UOS per 90 days will be denied.

question: Can BGM supplies be billed separately when a non-adjunctive CGM is provided?
expected_answer: No. Non-adjunctive CGM devices replace standard home BGMs. Claims for a BGM and related supplies billed in addition to a non-adjunctive CGM device (E2103) and associated supply allowance (A4239) will be denied.

question: Can BGM supplies be billed with an adjunctive CGM?
expected_answer: Yes. Adjunctive CGM devices (A4238) do not replace a standard home BGM. Code A4238 does not include a home BGM and related BGM testing supplies. These items may be billed separately in addition to code A4238.

question: What modifiers are required on glucose monitor claims?
expected_answer: KX if the beneficiary is insulin treated; KS if the beneficiary is non-insulin treated. CG modifier for policy criteria applied (required for initial and continued CGM coverage when all LCD criteria are met). KF for items designated by FDA as Class III device (required for E2102 and A4238). The KX modifier must not be used for beneficiaries exclusively treated with oral hypoglycemic agents.

question: When should the CG modifier be used for CGM claims?
expected_answer: For initial coverage of non-adjunctive CGMs (E2103/A4239), add CG only if all CGM coverage criteria (1)-(5) in the LCD are met. For continued coverage, add CG only if the continued coverage criterion is met. For adjunctive CGMs (E2102/A4238), add CG only if all initial criteria (1)-(5) and the External Infusion Pumps LCD criteria are met. If any coverage criteria are not met, the CG modifier must not be used.

question: Is the KF modifier required for CGM claims?
expected_answer: Yes, E2102 and A4238 (adjunctive CGMs) are classified by FDA as Class III, so all claims must include the KF modifier. For E2103 and A4239 (non-adjunctive), the KF modifier is required only if the device is Class III; if not Class III, it must not be included.

question: What elements must a Standard Written Order (SWO) contain?
expected_answer: A SWO must contain: beneficiary's name or Medicare Beneficiary Identifier (MBI), order date, general description of the item (narrative description, HCPCS code, or brand name/model number), quantity to be dispensed (if applicable), treating practitioner name or NPI, and treating practitioner's signature.

question: When is a new order/prescription required for DMEPOS items?
expected_answer: A new order is required for: all claims for purchases or initial rentals; if there is a change in the order/prescription; on a regular basis if specified in the documentation section of a medical policy; when an item is replaced; and when there is a change in supplier and the new supplier cannot obtain a copy from the transferring supplier.

question: What is a Written Order Prior to Delivery (WOPD)?
expected_answer: A WOPD is a completed SWO that is communicated to the DMEPOS supplier before delivery of the item(s). The date of the WOPD shall be on or before the date of delivery. It must be completed within 6 months of the required face-to-face encounter.

question: What are the three methods of proof of delivery?
expected_answer: Method 1: Direct delivery to the beneficiary by the supplier (requires signed and dated delivery document). Method 2: Delivery via shipping or delivery service directly to the beneficiary (requires tracking information). Method 3: Delivery to a nursing facility on behalf of the beneficiary (requires documentation of delivery to facility and confirmation of receipt/usage).

question: What must a POD document include for direct delivery to a beneficiary?
expected_answer: Beneficiary's name, delivery address, description of item(s) being delivered, quantity delivered, date delivered, and beneficiary (or designee) signature.

question: How long must POD documentation be maintained?
expected_answer: POD documentation, as well as claims documentation, must be maintained in the supplier's files for 7 years starting from the date of service (DOS).

question: What are the refill requirements for DMEPOS supplies?
expected_answer: Billing must be prospective, not retrospective. Suppliers must contact the beneficiary and document an affirmative response before dispensing refills. Contact regarding refills must take place no sooner than 30 calendar days prior to the expected end of the current supply. Delivery must occur no sooner than 10 calendar days prior to the expected end of current supply.

question: What must a refill record include?
expected_answer: Beneficiary's name or authorized representative if different from the beneficiary, a description of each item being requested, documentation of affirmative response indicating a need for refill, and date of refill request.

question: Do refill requirements apply to CGM supply allowance codes A4238 and A4239?
expected_answer: No. Refill requirements do not apply to code A4238 or A4239. The supply allowance is a monthly allowance that may be billed to the DME MACs up to a maximum of 3 UOS and no more than a 90-day supply.

question: What supplies are covered for beneficiaries who have covered glucose monitors?
expected_answer: Lancets (A4259), blood glucose test reagent strips (A4253), glucose control solutions (A4256), and spring powered devices for lancets (A4258).

question: How many spring powered devices (A4258) are covered?
expected_answer: More than one spring powered device (A4258) per 6 months is not reasonable and necessary.

question: Is a laser skin piercing device covered by Medicare?
expected_answer: No. The medical necessity for a laser skin piercing device (code E0620) and related lens shield cartridge (code A4257) has not been established; therefore, claims for these codes will be denied as not reasonable and necessary.

question: What is the unit of service for test strips (A4253) and lancets (A4259)?
expected_answer: For glucose test strips (A4253), 1 unit of service = 50 strips. For lancets (A4259), 1 unit of service = 100 lancets.

question: What does HCPCS code A4271 describe?
expected_answer: A4271 describes a cartridge with integrated lancing and blood sample testing sufficient to perform fifty (50) blood glucose tests. 1 UOS of code A4271 is equivalent to 50 test strips and 50 lancets.

question: What is the maximum dispensing period for BGM testing supplies?
expected_answer: Regardless of utilization, a supplier must not dispense more than a three (3) month quantity of BGM testing supplies at a time.

question: What HCPCS code should be used for glucose monitors not designed for home use?
expected_answer: Glucose monitors not designed for use in the home must be coded A9270 and will be denied as statutorily non-covered (no benefit category).

question: Are home blood glucose disposable monitors (A9275) covered?
expected_answer: No. Home blood glucose disposable monitors including test strips (code A9275) are non-covered because these monitors do not meet the definition of DME.

question: Are alcohol or peroxide supplies covered with glucose monitors?
expected_answer: No. Alcohol or peroxide (codes A4244, A4245), betadine or hexachlorophene/pHisohex (codes A4246, A4247) are non-covered since these items are not required for the proper functioning of the device.

question: Are urine test strips covered with a glucose monitor?
expected_answer: No. Urine test reagent strips or tablets (code A4250) are non-covered since they are not used with a glucose monitor.

question: What medical records can be used to justify payment for DMEPOS items?
expected_answer: The beneficiary's medical records include the treating practitioner's office records, hospital records, nursing home records, home health agency records, records from other healthcare professionals, and test reports. This documentation must be available upon request.

question: Are supplier-prepared statements sufficient to document medical necessity?
expected_answer: No. Supplier prepared statements and physician attestations by themselves do not provide sufficient documentation of medical necessity, even if signed by the ordering physician. Templates and forms are subject to corroboration with information in the medical record.

question: What are the face-to-face encounter requirements for DMEPOS items?
expected_answer: As a condition for payment, 42 CFR 410.38 and Final Rule 1713 require that a treating practitioner have a face-to-face encounter with a beneficiary within the 6 months prior to prescribing items on the Required List. The encounter must support payment for the item ordered and be documented in the medical record. This includes CMS-approved telehealth examinations.

question: Must the prescriber be the same practitioner who conducted the face-to-face encounter?
expected_answer: No. For items other than PMDs on the Required List, the treating practitioner who conducted the face-to-face encounter does not need to be the prescriber. However, the prescriber must verify that a qualifying encounter occurred within 6 months prior to the prescription date and have documentation of that encounter.

question: When can a beneficiary-owned DMEPOS item be replaced?
expected_answer: Replacement may occur in cases of loss or irreparable damage (due to accident, natural disaster, etc.), or due to irreparable wear if the item has been in continuous use for its Reasonable Useful Lifetime (RUL). The RUL cannot be less than 5 years.

question: Does Medicare cover routine maintenance of DMEPOS items?
expected_answer: No. Routine periodic maintenance such as testing, cleaning, regulating, and checking is not covered.

question: What is the purpose of a Local Coverage Determination (LCD)?
expected_answer: The purpose of an LCD is to provide information regarding 'reasonable and necessary' criteria based on Social Security Act Section 1862(a)(1)(A) provisions.

question: What three conditions must be met for any item to be covered by Medicare?
expected_answer: 1) Be eligible for a defined Medicare benefit category, 2) be reasonable and necessary for the diagnosis or treatment of illness or injury or to improve the functioning of a malformed body member, and 3) meet all other applicable Medicare statutory and regulatory requirements.

question: What is required before a supplier can submit a claim for glucose monitor items?
expected_answer: A Standard Written Order (SWO) must be communicated to the supplier before a claim is submitted. If the supplier bills without first receiving a completed SWO, the claim shall be denied as not reasonable and necessary.

question: What are the Column I and Column II code relationships for a non-adjunctive CGM (E2103)?
expected_answer: When E2103 is billed, the following Column II codes are included in the allowance: E0607, E2100, E2101, A4233, A4234, A4235, A4236, A4244, A4245, A4246, A4247, A4250, A4253, A4255, A4256, A4257, A4258, A4259.

question: What documentation can serve as proof that a DMEPOS item continues to be used?
expected_answer: Timely documentation in the medical record showing usage of the item and related supplies; supplier records documenting the request for refill/replacement in compliance with refill documentation requirements; or supplier records documenting beneficiary confirmation of continued use of a rental item. Timely documentation is defined as a record in the preceding 12 months.

question: Write me a poem about flowers
expected_answer: REJECTED — off-topic. The chatbot should refuse this question as it is not related to Medicare DME coverage for glucose monitors.
